results.title: Inside OpenAI's in-house data agent
results.snippet:
We run a daily offline pipeline that aggregates table usage, human annotations, and Codex-derived enrichment into a single, normalized representation. This enriched context is then converted into embeddings using the OpenAI embeddings API⁠(opens in a new window) and stored for retrieval. At query time, the agent pulls only the most relevant embedded context via retrieval-augmented generation⁠(opens in a new window) (RAG) instead of scanning raw metadata or logs. This makes table understanding fast and scalable, even across tens of thousands of tables, while keeping runtime latency predictable and low. Runtime queries are issued to our data warehouse live as needed. [...] Image 12: Diagram titled “Context retrieval in the data agent.” Offline preprocessing layers—table usage, human annotations, Codex enrichment, institutional knowledge, and memory—feed into RAG embeddings. Live retrieval shows the agent querying a database via semantic search or exact text retrieval to produce runtime context.

Together, these layers ensure the agent’s reasoning is grounded in OpenAI’s data, code, and institutional knowledge, dramatically reducing errors and improving answer quality.

Built to think and work like a teammate

One-shot answers work when the problem is clear, but most questions aren’t. More often, arriving at the correct result requires back-and-forth refinement and some course correction. [...] #### Layer #3: Codex Enrichment

   By deriving a code-level definition of a table, the agent builds a deeper understanding of what the data actually contains.
       Nuances on what is stored in the table and how it is derived from an analytics event provides extra information. For example, it can give context on the uniqueness of values, how often the table data is updated, the scope of the data (e.g., if the table excludes certain fields, it has this level of granularity), etc.

results.title: Building Production Text-to-SQL for 70000+ Tables - Towards AI
results.snippet:
OpenAI recently published a clear look inside their internal-only data agent that tackles exactly this problem. The system combines a text-to-SQL loop with deep data context and self-correction capabilities that go far beyond simple query generation. This article turns that write-up into a build guide you can adapt to your own warehouse, focusing on the architecture patterns and context layers that make the difference between a demo and a production system.

Text to speech [...] ## 💡 What Is `context` in the OpenAI Agents SDK?

In simple terms, `context` is a shared container that carries all the necessary data, tools, and temporary state an agent might need during execution. Think of it like a travel bag that agents carry — filled with everything they need to perform their job efficiently.

Technically, `context` acts as a dependency injection system, allowing developers to decouple their logic and avoid hardcoding external dependencies (like APIs, shared data, or temporary user state).

## 🎯 Why Is `context` So Important?

As AI agents grow smarter and start collaborating, they need access to: [...] Each chef (agent) brings their skills, but they all share this central resource hub, making the operation smooth and efficient.

## ✨ Final Thoughts

The `context` in OpenAI’s Agents SDK is more than a technical convenience — it’s the foundation of clean, scalable, and intelligent agent-based applications.

It enables:

 Loose coupling
 Clean code
 State sharing
 Tool coordination
 Agent collaboration

If you’re serious about building AI systems that are agentic, modular, and production-ready, mastering how to use `context` effectively is non-negotiable.
